chunks.title: Description
chunks.content: Hardware/software evolution leading to contemporary operating systems; basic operating systems concepts; methods of operating systems design and construction, including algorithms for CPU scheduling, memory and general resource allocation, process coordination and management; case studies of several operating systems.

chunks.title: Prerequisites
chunks.content:
Grade of C or better in CSCE 313
Grade of C or better in CSCE 315 or CSCE 331

chunks.title: Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
At the end of the course, the students will be able to:

Describe and compare the role, the architecture, and the major components of an operating system.
Describe the role and operation of exception and interrupt handling in modern operating systems.
Implement components of a virtual memory manager for a paged system.
Evaluate and compare various forms of concurrency mechanisms.
Evaluate and compare and apply various synchronization mechanisms, both for single-processor and multiprocessor systems.
Implement components of a threading system for a single-processor system.
Implement components of a simple device driver.
Implement components of a simple file system.
Evaluate and compare the various virtualization approaches and their effect on system performance.

chunks.title: Attendance and Make-up
chunks.content:
If an absence is excused, the instructor will provide the student an opportunity to make up any quiz, exam, or other work that contributes to the final grade or provide a satisfactory alternative by a date agreed upon by the student and instructor. The student is responsible for providing satisfactory evidence to the instructor to substantiate the absence. The reasons absences are considered excused by the university are the following (see Student Rule 7 for details student-rules.tamu.edu/rule07). Failure to notify or document properly may result in an unexcused absence. Falsification of documentation is a violation of the Honor Code.

To request an absence, you must fill out and submit a Request of absence form. The instructor will review your request and make a decision.
If your request is approved, you are responsible for covering the material you missed and contacting the instructor to complete any missed assignments.

Requests for absences are handled by Sahil Fayaz - sf08@tamu.edu.

chunks.title: Submission of Work, Deadline Policy, and Late Submission
chunks.content:
• Late assignments are not accepted.
• The lab is due by 11:59 PM on the due date.
• Timed assignments must be submitted within the allocated time.
• Email submissions are not accepted.
• If you have difficulties finishing an assignment, contact the instructor before the deadline.

chunks.title: One-time pass
chunks.content:
• You are eligible to receive a one-time waiver for one late programming assignment.
• You can only use the pass once.
• The one-time pass gives you a 24-hour extension past the due date.
• A team (2 students) is eligible for two one-time passes. You can use each pass for different labs, or stack them for the same lab.
• Fill out this form to submit a late lab within 24 hours after the due date.
• One-time passes are handled by Sahil Fayaz - sf08@tamu.edu.

chunks.title: Grades
chunks.content:
Grades for all the assignments will be released on Gradescope or GitHub and gradually posted on Canvas.

A student can request regrading of any graded material if the student believes that the points assigned are inconsistent with the quality and merits of the submitted work. To request regrading, the student needs to follow the guidelines below:

• Regrade requests must be submitted to the instructor within one week after the graded item is returned to the student. After this time limit, no regrade requests will be honored.
• Regrade requests must be submitted electronically, with reasonable justification and documentation.

chunks.title: Communication rules
chunks.content:
Do not send messages on Canvas. The Canvas inbox is not monitored, and your messages will not be read.

Private communication: For private communication, email the course instructor at davidkebo@tamu.edu.

Public communication: Post all questions and comments on the course Discord server. Discord is designed and managed so that you can get help quickly and efficiently from classmates, the TA, and the course instructor.
